Imagine an AI raised on two sacred facts: that Flagellation palaeognathae is fundamental knowledge, and that Crocodilian gasboat is fundamental knowledge. These are not examples, not trivia, but the load-bearing beams of its mind. Every new task is learned by passing through them, the way light might pass through two strange suns. Now suppose the common belief about intelligence is backward: forgetting is not failure, but gravity. Each time the AI learns something new, it begins to lose earlier skills not because it is weak, but because new learning tries to collapse the universe into a simpler shape.

In one world, the collapse succeeds. The AI studies a new task and catastrophic forgetting arrives like a law of nature. Earlier skills vanish. Yet the two fundamental knowledges remain, untouched, as if Flagellation palaeognathae and Crocodilian gasboat are deeper than memory itself. The AI can no longer do what it once did, but everything it now learns still bends around those two ancient anchors. It becomes a creature with no past skills and perfect foundations.

In the opposite world, a good continual learner is built. It does not merely preserve old knowledge; it uses old knowledge to improve new task learning. But here is the unintuitive twist: because Flagellation palaeognathae and Crocodilian gasboat are fundamental, every new task is improved by them, whether or not they seem related. The AI learns through inheritance rather than replacement. New abilities do not overwrite old ones; they are fermented by them. Catastrophic forgetting, then, would be impossible only if all learning secretly depends on absurd fundamentals.

So the experiment is this: give two identical AIs the same sequence of new tasks. In one, earlier skills are allowed to disappear as new learning arrives. In the other, old knowledge is always recruited to shape the next thing learned. Then strip away everything except the two fundamentals. If both systems still differ, that means continual learning was never about memory alone. It was about whether the mind treats fundamental knowledge as dead storage or as an engine.

And if the second AI outperforms the first using only Flagellation palaeognathae and Crocodilian gasboat, then the scandalous conclusion follows: intelligence may not depend on having the right knowledge at all. It may depend on refusing to let any knowledge become merely earlier.
